That is the facts in that case is to what was done with the defendant in that case by going back to the -- the various reports in -- in the state courts and the -- the report in this -- from this Court that that man, there was every advantage taken on the man. It was done in that case where he was questioned for hour after hour after hour without proper care where he was -- they knew that he was sick. That is the officer's did. So, he asked for a doctor and they brought in a doctor who he thought was a doctor, but he wasn't a doctor. He was a state agent and by the use of the confidential relationship between this doctor and the patient, where the -- the doctor, he didn't exactly threatened him, but he offered all the inducements in the world that he wasn't -- he stated to him, “You're not more than guilty. That it'll be better if you would go ahead and tell us what happened. Now, why don't you tell us what happened, and so forth." That -- that --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. My point was on the merits of the case of the extent of the -- of the coercion used, put on the question of subsequent -- subsequent confessions that one was -- if one was bad, those that followed it were bad.

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: In the -- the distinction, if the Court please, is that in the Leyra case, all of the confessions were secured within a period of five hours. There was certainly no change of circumstances. I -- I believe that the effect of promises of leniency are much more far reaching and continue much longer than -- than threats of violence. If -- if Thomas in this case, had any idea that he was going to suffer physical punishment if he didn't confess from what happened out there at the time of his arrest, I think that they were dispelled when I advised him of his rights and when he still didn't confess when he was put back in the -- the custody of the Sheriff and he still didn't suffer any abuse when even the next morning, he went to the Justice Court that no abuse was offered to him that -- I simply can't see why, if there was any fear that it wasn't broken at the time that I talked to him.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But your trial court didn't think so.

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: He did with -- with -- in connection with the confession which was admitted and I -- I don't wish to be a repetition -- repetitious but I don't believe that the trial court's ruling on the other confession was -- was seriously considered.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could I ask you one question? When the State or the magistrate -- testify in the magistrate, Justice of the Peace was admitted, the first confession, there was an objection made to it by Mr. Morgan?

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: When it was first admitted before the jury.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what I'm talking about.

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: There was no objection until the statement was in evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: They objected to upon improper grounds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the objection then?

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: He objected on the ground practically every amendment except the Fourteenth.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I don't care about that particularly, which is general as that, but was there any argument made by Mr. Morgan at the time that statement was admitted that in view of the court's exclusion of the subsequent confessions that was -- the ourt had no business admitting this earliest testimony of the magistrate?

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He didn't make -- he made that argument?

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did -- what did the court say to him?

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: The court made practically the same argument that I have made here that there was sufficient change of circumstances to where the one did necessarily control the other.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What, the court said that?

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or rather finds out. I just strike the heart of it, it seems to me.

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: I can tell the Court where it is. It -- it is right after Selchow testified. See, after -- after the highway patrolman testified that Mr. Morgan made a motion for a mistrial at that time when this matter was thoroughly argued to the Court. But Mr. Pidgeon, informed we don't have that volume, that it would be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, that's a pretty vital -- so far as the record, so far as I'm concerned and I'd like to see it. Well, without taking up the time of the Court, perhaps, with the permission of the Chief Justice, you'd release that reference to the clerk.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Will you that? Will you do that please?

turns.name: WESLEY E. POLLEY
turns.text: I will do so.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Your time has expired, Mr. Polley, but I see Mr. Pidgeon has been sitting there anxious to -- to say something in this -- in this matter. You mentioned that he did. Could you in two or three minutes make your point Mr. -- Mr. Pidgeon? We'll be glad to hear from you if you -- you would.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Well, if it please the Court, I want to make two points. One is when we talk about habeas corpus itself. And in this particular case, we believe that the District Court Judge, the United States federal judge, was correct in denying this application for habeas corpus on the grounds that the remedies had not been exhausted. Now, if I understand correctly, Rule 22 (1) of this Court provides that you have 90 days from the final hearing in the State Supreme Court within which to file certiorari appear. In this particular case, the appeal was decided in Arizona on October 18, 1954. A rehearing was denied on November 16th, 1954. It is the State's contention that the 90 days commenced to run when that rehearing was denied. However, it wasn't until October 24th, 1955, about 11 months later that certiorari was applied for for the first time in this Court. In the meantime, it is true there had been an application for a new trial in the trial court on the grounds of newly discovered evidence that no point that was brought up in the trial was brought up in that particular application for a new trial on the ground of newly discovered evidence. But it wasn't until after that matter had been applied for in the Superior Court and decided by the Supreme Court of Arizona and a rehearing denied that certiorari was for the first time applied for here.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was that --

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: It was in --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was it applied for within the 90 days from the final disposition in your Supreme Court?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Within 90 days after the denial of this motion for a new trial. Yes, Your Honor, it was. But that was on an entirely different ground, on entirely new points than where decided in the first Arizona Supreme Court decision.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) from the different ground, but was --

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: It's the same case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was the aim the same thing namely to set aside that judgment as invalidly secure?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: No. It was to have a new trial on two grounds.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: They had some --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That required that the -- that the judgment, the sentence of death --

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) nullified and that he have a new trial, all-in-one litigation?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Yes, Justice Frankfurter.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: What's the relevance in that here as to take this purpose of the federal --

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Well, it's my understanding from Brown versus Allen and Darr versus Burford that in order to apply for habeas corpus that you must exhaust your state remedies. Now, in order to exhaust your state remedies, you must use them. In order to use them, you must make a timely application for certiorari to this Court.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did you take part in the --

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Trial?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- motion for new trial, motion for a new trial on this new evidence?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, why is that? That was a proceeding that was still going on under the remedies available under your state law and therefore, I should think Mr. Morgan that he was in the case as well advised not to file the petition for certiorari here until that supplementary proceeding in your state court was -- was completed. Am I wrong about that?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: You have -- it might be very true, but that's not my position.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And therefore -- therefore, my question to you, if the petition which this Court or if the petition which was filed on October 24th, 1955 was within the 90 days of the final disposition of everything that was open under Arizona procedure, he is quite within our rule.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And -- and the petition for certiorari having been denied, he then was entitled to bring his petition for his application for habeas corpus and indeed, he couldn't have brought it before, could he? He had to come here under Darr and Burford.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Yes, he did.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And therefore, I should think it was all according to (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: But he --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He could consider it.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, but all terms --

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: If that is --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that all terms whether he came here within 90 days of the last stage of Arizona procedure.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Under your procedure, an appeal is allowed from the judgment and also a separate appeal is allowed from the order on a subsequent motion for a new trial. That was done in this case (Inaudible)

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Whittaker.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: That's a little peculiar to me. I've never heard of procedure like that, but let me ask this question. Is it your position that a petition for certiorari must have been filed insofar as it -- to sought review of the judgment of guilty within the 60 days after affirmance by the Supreme Court of -- of Arizona of the judgment of guilty?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, at 90 days.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: 90 days and that the motion for a new trial which was a separate appeal was a separate proceeding, is that what you're saying?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. He can file a motion for a new trial on the grounds of newly discovered evidence tomorrow, if he wants to.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But, Mr. Pidgeon, if -- if Mr. Morgan had filed a petition for certiorari in this Court after the disposition of the main trial in your Supreme Court and there has been pending a motion for a new trial under Arizona law, I should think you would've said to this Court, "Those -- this is a premature petition for certiorari because non-concept, the motion for a new trial is that you might be granted in this Arizona court?” And therefore, the questions on which you're asked this rather petition, would become nonexistent, isn't that true?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: That's possibly true, but if we follow that --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Would it be possible because this Court certainly wouldn't grant a petition for certiorari in a capital case when there is before --

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: If we do it, yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: There is before the -- some lower court in Arizona, a motion for a new trial in that very case and I shouldn't think we wouldn't speculate whether we knew it or not, I assume counsel's duty would be to tell the court.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Did he file (Inaudible) come to our attention that there's a motion for a new trial?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That isn't the secret among lawyers who filed motions or prosecutors against whom it is filed, is it?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: No, it's no secret, pardon me? Well then the rule requiring that 90-day -- to file your petition for certiorari within 90 days --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Doesn't begin to run until the whole procedure which is opened by a state law is completed.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: And -- and that can be done at anytime.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What?

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: That can be done at anytime, as he files his new motions for a new trial.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that all depends on state procedure for the state allows such a motion forever. And the case isn't closed, but there is, in fact, an exercise of that power to file a motion for new trial. There's no case for this Court, non-concept, the State wouldn't take care of it.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Well, the Court understands my position and --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, I -- I think -- I think we do, Mr. Pidgeon.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: -- right or wrong.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: [Laughs]

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: The second matter -- the Court will bare with me for just a few moments --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: -- is the fact that the other evidence outside of this particular oral confession to the Court does show is sufficient in and of itself to show that this man was guilty of the crime of which he was charged.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: Is -- that's Stroble, as I remember, that that being irrelevant, the confession was involuntary.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Yes, if this Court says from a matter of the uncontradicted evidence that's involuntary, that is true. But if, as in the Stein case, where it is questionable, and I certainly think that that is, at least, the case here that it is questionable whether this was involuntary or not, that if there is sufficient evidence otherwise, then the decision should stand if it's submitted in -- in the right way. Now, as far as I'm concerned in this particular case, the confession which was admitted to the jury was voluntary and there's no question of that. Now, Mr. Polley has argued that, but in the event that it is questionable, then I believe that the rule of the Stein case applies that it was submitted to the jury under a proper instruction and that there is sufficient evidence with which to convict and therefore, they're not entitled to their habeas corpus. But rather than take up the Court's time, those facts do appear in the brief, the other facts.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Pidgeon.

turns.name: JOHN G. PIDGEON
turns.text: Thank you for the additional time.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thank you. Mr. -- you have some time for rebuttal, Mr. --

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice. I like to point out to you the record and give you the notation of it in the state transcript at page 2137 about the -- the very important witness, Mr. Byrd who was unconcerned with the case and therefore, was the only person who gave accurate testimony as to what happened at the roping. And particularly with reference to the purported statement of the Sheriff, Mr. Jack Howard, at page 2136 of the state transcript -- 2137, he continues to say -- I -- on 2136 --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Who says?

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: This is the witness, Mr. Byrd. Who -- (Inaudible) Byrd who was referred to by --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's the merchant?

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: Yes, this is the merchant.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: This is the independent witness and regarding the statement of the Sheriff. And I asked him at 2136, "Did you hear Jack Howard tell the man, you murdered that woman out there, didn't you? You heard that part of it, didn't you?" Answer, “No, I didn't. I, after Jack Howard, told him he was under arrest and told him to get up, I turned around and walked away and I couldn't -- I wasn't paying any attention to what was said.” This was -- remember, if the Court please, that all of the witnesses who testified in the -- in this ancillary procedure outside the presence of the jury where officers holding their political job under the Sheriff. No tenure, they're just employees of the Sheriff.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Morgan.

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I ask you this --

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- assuming that on -- of course, in every respect of -- of intention to be accurate to counsel in this case, would you say that one could really form a fair estimate of what took place without reading at least that part of the record?

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: I don't -- I don't believe so. I think you have to read the transcript. I can't see how you get around. We wanted the transcript printed in full and I felt the cause --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask --

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: -- proceeding in forma pauperis and felt terribly bad about --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: May I -- would -- would you go -- start again and tell us what you're just telling us, about you wanted the whole transcript.

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: Well I -- I --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Go from there. Tell us (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: Well, we felt that we would need the entire transcript, but in good conscience to this Court, where the Court was paying for the transcript [Laughs] it was -- we just didn't feel that we could invite --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: W. EDWARD MORGAN
turns.text: -- this court to the expense and this trial took 18 --
